coredata.dc:description: Deep eutectic solvents (DES), renowned for their exceptional solubility and compatibility, have been explored as effective solvents and dopants for carbon dots (CDs) synthesis. However, their application is often restricted by requirements for high pressure or temperature. This paper introduces a simple method for utilizing DES in processing lignocellulose to synthesize CDs under mild conditions at 110°C. The synthesized CDs exhibited excellent fluorescence characteristics, and maximum emission wavelengths ranging from 360 nm to 550 nm. Notably, the recycling of DES enhanced the performance of the CDs. Additionally, the study revealed discrepancies in the carbon precursors and the synthesis mechanisms of the CDs obtained from different DES systems. The synthesized CDs demonstrated great potential for detecting Fe3 + in water, with a limit of detection as low as 191 ppm. Furthermore, these CDs can be utilized in inks and the production of flexible composite films due to stable optical properties and exceptional chemical characteristics. This approach provides a new prospect for the synthesis and regulation of CDs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Deep eutectic solvents (DES), renowned for their exceptional solubility and compatibility, have been explored as effective solvents and dopants for carbon dots (CDs) synthesis. However, their application is often restricted by requirements for high pressure or temperature. This paper introduces a simple method for utilizing DES in processing lignocellulose to synthesize CDs under mild conditions at 110°C. The synthesized CDs exhibited excellent fluorescence characteristics, and maximum emission wavelengths ranging from 360 nm to 550 nm. Notably, the recycling of DES enhanced the performance of the CDs. Additionally, the study revealed discrepancies in the carbon precursors and the synthesis mechanisms of the CDs obtained from different DES systems. The synthesized CDs demonstrated great potential for detecting Fe3 + in water, with a limit of detection as low as 191 ppm. Furthermore, these CDs can be utilized in inks and the production of flexible composite films due to stable optical properties and exceptional chemical characteristics. This approach provides a new prospect for the synthesis and regulation of CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, fluorescent materials have been intensively studied due to their numerous advantages, including exceptional photostability and diverse applications (Zhu et al., 2022a). Among these materials, carbon dots (CDs) are particularly noteworthy due to their various preparation methods, abundant raw materials, excellent water solubility, and unique fluorescence properties. They are seen as a potential solution to the challenges faced by semiconductor quantum dots and offer a wide range of applications in fields such as catalysis, sensing, anti-counterfeiting, and biomedicine (Abdollahi et al., 2020; Cai et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2017; Wareing et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2018). The sources of raw materials used to prepare CDs are diverse, ranging from chemicals to natural biomass (Gan et al., 2023; Peng et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2018; Zheng et al., 2019). However, certain carbon sources are costly and involve complex synthesis processes, which restrict the expansion of further research. Lignocellulose, a sustainable and environmentally friendly resource, has emerged as a potential large-scale and low-cost precursor for CDs (Abbas et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2018; Woo et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2020b). The approaches for synthesizing CDs include chemical oxidation, microwave irradiation, and hydrothermal or solvothermal reaction. However, these methods often suffer from high temperature and high-pressure reaction conditions, the use of strong acids and bases, multiple processing steps, and intricate separation techniques (Hu et al., 2020; Tang et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022b; Wu et al., 2023). To enhance the optical properties of CDs, passivating agents or dopants are often employed. Sulfuric, nitric, or phosphoric acids are commonly utilized in the synthesis of CDs to modulate the energy band structure and optimize their optoelectronic properties (Yang et al., 2020a; Zhou et al., 2018). Unfortunately, the use of these additives is constrained by their highly corrosive, toxic, and oxidizing properties. Therefore, there is a strong desire for a synthesis method for fluorescent CDs that is easy to operate, environmentally friendly, and cost-effective.

Deep eutectic solvents (DES) are an emerging class of designer solvents with advantages such as ease of preparation, low cost, recyclability, and tunable properties. Recently, DES have been utilized as both solvents and dopants in the synthesis of CDs. The synthesized DES was mixed with a carbon source to produce CDs through solvothermal or microwave methods (Jiang et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2022a). However, relatively rigorous synthesis conditions are still required. DES can effectively break down plant cell walls, enabling efficient separation of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin components. Notably, the process of deconstructing lignocellulose in DES also includes the depolymerization of certain components, leading to the formation of oligomers or small molecules (Xu et al., 2023). These small molecules or oligomers also undergo recombination, polymerization, and carbonization reactions during the process. Several studies have reported the production of lignin nanoparticles (LNPs) during this process (Guo et al., 2022). Nevertheless, to the best of the authors’ knowledge, there are no reports on whether fluorescent CDs are produced during lignocellulose treatment with DES.

This study aimed to synthesize fluorescent CDs using various precursor feedstocks and eight types of DESs under mild conditions. The resulting CDs exhibit emission wavelengths ranging from 360 to 550 nm and particle sizes of approximately 2–5 nm. The structural and optical properties of CDs obtained by treating the primary components of lignocellulose with acidic or alkaline DES were analyzed. Discrepancies were observed in the carbon precursors and synthesis mechanisms of the CDs obtained from different solvent treatments. The DES was recycled three times, which enhanced the fluorescence properties of the CDs. The CDs also exhibit favorable dispersibility and photostability, holding significant potential for application in fluorescent sensing of metal ions, information storage, and anti-counterfeiting encryption. The simultaneous synthesis of CDs during the lignocellulose deconstruction process using DES offers a promising new direction for the production and regulation of CDs.

Balsa and bamboo were obtained from a wood processing plant (Fujian Province, China). Mongolian pine was purchased from Lvlin Wood Industry Co., Ltd. (Jiangsu, China). All of them were dried, milled, and screened to achieve a particle size ranging from 20 to 80 mesh. Xylan and paddle board were obtained from a viscose fiber mill (Xinjiang, China), and enzymatically hydrolyzed lignin was supplied by Shandong Longlive Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. Choline chloride (ChCl), formic acid (FA), oxalate dihydrate (OD), m-phenylenediamine (MPD), ethylene glycol (EG), ethanolamine (EA), aluminium chloride hexahydrate (ACH), Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA-1799), ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), potassium chloride (KCl), calcium chloride (CaCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), manganese chloride (MnCl2), ferric chloride (FeCl3), ferrous chloride (FeCl2), barium chloride (BaCl2) and lithium chloride (LiCl) were purchased from Macklin Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glycerin (Gly) and anhydrous ethanol were provided by Modern Oriental Technology Development Co., Ltd. Lactic acid (LA) and p-toluene sulfonic acid (PTSA) were purchased from Shandong West Asia Chemical Co., Ltd. Commercial bacterial cellulose film (BC) was obtained from Hainan Yide Food Industry Co., Ltd. All the chemicals used were of analytical grade.

DES was prepared by blending hydrogen bond acceptors (HBA) and hydrogen bond donors (HBD) in appropriate molar ratios ( Table 1). The mixture was then heated and stirred at 80°C until a homogeneous and transparent liquid was achieved. Subsequently, 4 g balsa and 60 g DES were placed in a beaker and heated to 110 °C for 5 h. After the reaction was completed, 100 mL ethanol/water (3:1 v/v) was added to the slurry and then filtered. The filtrate was concentrated and mixed with 5 volumes of deionized water. After removing the solid, the supernatant was concentrated to obtain the recovered DES (ReDES). ReDES was further subjected to dialysis using a 1000 Da molecular weight cutoff for 3 days and filtered with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to obtain a solution of CDs. The recovered ReDES and CDs were labeled as ReDES1 and CDs1. The process was repeated to obtain CDs2 and CDs3 using ReDES as the solvent. Pulp board, industrial xylan and enzymatic hydrolysis lignin were employed to synthesize C-CDs, H-CDs and L-CDs, respectively, following the same protocol. The CDs derived from the A-DES treatment were designated as A-CDs, with other samples following the same nomenclature. As a control, DES was directly used to synthesize DES-CDs. Detailed explorations of the effect of Al3+ on the optics of A-CDs are provided in the Supplementary Material (ESI).

The effect of K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Ba2+, and Li+ on the fluorescence of CDs was investigated. Specifically, solutions containing 1 mL of different metal ions (25 ppm) were prepared. Subsequently, 2 mL of the CDs solution was added, and the Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the solutions were measured using a spectrophotometer. The fluorescence intensity was recorded at 530 nm, with an excitation wavelength of 440 nm. Fe3+ caused the maximum quenching in PL intensity. Therefore, further sensing experiments were conducted using solutions containing Fe3+ at concentrations ranging from 0.5 to 200 ppm.

The resulting fluorescence intensity data were plotted on a Stern-Volmer plot, with the fluorescence decay ratio (F0/F) on the vertical coordinate. Here, F0 represents the initial fluorescence intensity in the absence of quenching, and F represents the fluorescence intensity in the presence of quenching. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated using the following equation:
(1)
LOD =
3 × standard deviation
slope

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the anti-counterfeiting ink and composite film
ce:section.ce:para: 2 g of PVA was added to 20 mL of deionized water and stirred at 90°C until a clear solution was obtained. Then, 10 mL of the PVA solution was mixed with 10 mL of a CDs dispersion (10 mg/mL) and stirred at 25°C for 30 min to prepare the anti-counterfeit ink. PVA inks were directly synthesized as a control for comparative analysis. The resulting ink was transferred into a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) mold and dried at 60°C for 24 h to form a PVA-CDs composite film.

The BC was immersed in M-CDs solution and deionized water, then placed in a vacuum desiccator for 12 h. After drying at room temperature, the BC-M-CDs film and BC film were obtained. These films were then folded to create crafts.

The morphologies of CDs were observed using a High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEMF200, JEOL, Japan), and particle size statistics were analyzed with ImageJ software. The HRTEM images were processed with Gatan digital micrograph software to produce Fast fourier transform (FFT) images. The functional groups of the CDs and REDESs were recorded with a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Bruker, Germany). Raman scattering spectra were used to analyze the CDs, with a scattering range of 1000–2000 cm−1. The absorption and fluorescence spectra of the CDs were collected at room temperature using an UV–visible spectrometer (T9, Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd., China) and a Fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-7100, Japan), respectively. Fluorescence lifetime measurement of the CDs was conducted using a Transient/steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS1000, England). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the CDs samples were acquired using an X-ray diffractometer operated at a tube voltage and current of 40 kV and 40 mA, respectively. The elemental content and functional group composition of the CD surfaces were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-Alpha, Thermo Scientific, USA). The 1H and 13C NMR spectra of DES and REDES were obtained in DMSO-d6 using an AVIII 400 MHz spectrometer (Bruker, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Deconstruction of lignocellulose using DES under mild conditions
ce:section.ce:para: In the present study, the DES treatment under mild conditions (110 ℃, 5 h) resulted in the fractionation of lignocellulose into cellulose-rich residues, regenerated lignin, and a recovered DES solution containing CDs and trace amounts of polysaccharide fragments. After dialysis and filtration, the CDs were obtained. The experimental process is illustrated in Fig. 1, and the fluorescence emission spectra are presented in Fig. S1. CDs synthesized through various DES exhibited consistent emission wavelengths, primarily ranging from 350 and 450 nm. Notably, the CDs derived from M-DES displayed a distinct green fluorescence emission, with a maximum emission wavelength of 550 nm. This was largely attributed to the intrinsic functional groups of meta-phenylenediamine. Four types of CDs were selected for further characterization based on the acidic and alkaline DES used during their synthesis, as well as their specific fluorescence properties. The acidic types include ACH-Gly-CDs (A-CDs) and ChCl-FA-CDs (F-CDs), while the alkaline types involved ChCl-EA-CDs (E-CDs) and MPD-EG-CDs (M-CDs).

TEM and HRTEM were employed to analyze the size, morphology, and hyperfine structures of CDs. Fig. 2 showed that A-CDs, F-CDs, E-CDs, and M-CDs were homogeneous, widely dispersed, and free of any evident aggregation. The average dimensions of the CDs were measured to be 3.90, 3.49, 2.36, and 3.96 nm, respectively. The HRTEM images revealed that all four types of CDs exhibited distinct and well-defined lattice fringes, with a consistent spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon (Ding et al., 2018; Song et al., 2022). Based on these findings, it is evident that deconstructing lignocellulose using DES under mild conditions effectively yielded CDs. Furthermore, CDs were synthesized through DES treatment of coniferous wood, gramineous plants, and wood chips. The fluorescence properties, shown in Fig. S2, indicate that upon excitation at 365 nm, all synthesized CDs exhibited comparable fluorescence emission. This consistency suggested that the type of lignocellulose feedstock did not significantly influence the synthesis of CDs. It further implied that the prepared DES can effectively facilitate the synthesis of CDs during the pretreatment of lignocellulose.

FT-IR, Raman, XRD and XPS measurements were applied to analyze the functional groups and chemical structures of CDs. The FT-IR results (Fig. 2i) showed that the absorption peaks at approximately 3410 cm−1, were attributed to the stretching vibrations of O-H or N-H groups (Zhang et al., 2016). All the examined CDs exhibited absorption bands in the range of 1600–1660 cm−1, which were ascribed to CC or CN bond stretching vibrations. The C-N stretching vibration at 1250 cm−1 was observed in F-CDs, E-CDs and M-CDs. Besides, absorptions at 1430 cm−1 and 1100 cm−1 referred to C-H and C-O-C stretching vibrations, respectively (Xu et al., 2021). This analysis demonstrated that all lignocellulose-based CDs contain numerous polar bonds, including hydroxyl or amino groups. The Raman spectra ( Fig. 3h) indicated the degree of graphitization of the CDs, with characteristic peaks centered at ∼1380 cm−1 and ∼1550 cm−1, corresponding to the D and G bands of carbon materials. These bands represented disordered structures or defects and graphitic carbon domains, respectively. The intensity ratios (ID/IG) for A-CDs, F-CDs, and E-CDs were 0.97, 0.93, and 0.96, respectively, indicating similar graphitic carbon domains and a certain degree of graphitization (Li et al., 2022a; Verma et al., 2019). Furthermore, the XRD pattern of the CDs showed broad peaks near 26.5° and 21° (Fig. 2k), indicating the presence of both graphene and amorphous structures. The analysis revealed that the synthesized CDs comprise graphitic carbon nuclei with numerous surface functional groups.

The wide-scan XPS analysis revealed four prominent peaks at 75, 285, 400, and 532 eV, corresponding to the elements Al, C, N, and O, respectively. These results demonstrated that the primary composition of the CDs includes C, O, and Al/N components (Gu et al., 2021). To further validate the surface composition of the CDs, high-resolution XPS was acquired for the C 1 s, Al 2p, and N 1 s regions, as shown in Fig. 3a-h. In A-CDs, the C 1 s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three primary peaks at 284.78, 286.52, and 288.86 eV, corresponding to C-C/CC, C-O, and CO, respectively (Ding et al., 2015). The Al 2p spectrum exhibited a peak at 74.68 eV, indicating the presence of Al-O bonds in the A-CDs (Jayaweera et al., 2019). The detection of aluminum in A-CDs indicated that the aluminum chloride hexahydrate/propanediol deep eutectic solvent (A-DES) not only facilitated the deconstruction of lignocellulosic biomass but also contributed to the synthesis of CDs. The fine N 1 s spectra of E-CDs, F-CDs and M-CDs exhibit distinct peaks at approximately 399.40, 400.20 and 401.50 eV, attributed to amino N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively, indicating that N is effectively integrated with the CDs (Nie et al., 2014).

Additionally, the atomic concentrations and functional group percentages in the CDs were determined, with the proportions of each component shown in Table 2 and S1. The findings indicated that A-CDs exhibited a low C/O ratio, which may be due to more Al-O bonds, resulting in a high concentration of O element. The high percentage of C-C/CC bonds in A-CDs was primarily attributed to the intense treatment in the A-DES system, leading to a higher degree of sp2 hybridization, which was consistent with the Raman and XRD results. The C/O ratio of M-CDs was lower than that of M-DES-CDs, attributed to the presence of specific oxygen-containing functional groups from lignocellulose in the formation of CDs. Distinct differences in N content and bonding configurations among F-CDs, E-CDs, M-CDs and M-DES-CDs significantly influenced the coloration properties of CDs (Silva et al., 2021). The investigation confirmed that the surface of CDs contained a significant number of oxygen- or nitrogen-containing chemical groups. These polar groups contributed to their high-water solubility, enhancing their luminescent performance (Gao et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2023).

The optical properties of CDs were investigated by UV–vis absorption and Fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 4a-c, A-CDs, F-CDs and E-CDs exhibited a distinct absorption peak in the range of 250–275 nm, which corresponded to the π-π* electron transition of the CC bond in the sp2 carbon cluster. A weak absorption peak at 320 nm predominantly correlated with the n-π* transition of the CO bond (Schneider et al., 2016). The M-CDs displayed a distinct UV–vis absorption peak at 288 nm, which was attributed to the conjugated π domain of m-phenylenediamine (Fig. 4d and S3). Notably, the excitation spectrum overlapped with the absorption spectrum, confirming that the fluorescence emission was linked to the intrinsic functional group of m-phenylenediamine. M-CDs also exhibited an absorption peak at 208 nm, which may be associated with lignin. In order to verify the optical characteristics of CDs, photoluminescence measurements were carried out at various excitation wavelengths ranging from 240 to 460 nm with an interval of 20 nm. Fig. 4a-c clearly demonstrate the emission characteristics of A-CDs, F-CDs and E-CDs, which exhibited an excitation wavelength-dependent feature. Specifically, the emission wavelength gradually redshifted as the excitation wavelength increased. This phenomenon could be related to the different emission potential wells on the surface of CDs or their size effect (Ding et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2021). The optimal excitation wavelength for all three CDs was 360 nm, with a maximum emission wavelength at around 430 nm. The 3D photoluminescence contour plots in Fig. 4e-g illustrate that the emissions of all three CDs were primarily concentrated in the blue region. The inset in Fig. 4 highlights that the CDs in aqueous media appear faintly yellow under daylight but exhibit a bright blue under 365 nm irradiation. In addition, the maximum fluorescence excitation and emission wavelengths of M-CDs were longer, measured at 440 nm and 530 nm, respectively. Fig. 4h displays the CIE coordinate (0.36, 0.59) of M-CDs under an excitation wavelength of 440 nm. Fig. S3 and S4 show that M-DES-CDs and M-CDs exhibited comparable excitation and emission spectra. However, distinct differences in fluorescence intensities were observed at the same concentration and excitation wavelength, with M-CDs demonstrating substantially higher fluorescence intensities than M-DES-CDs. This difference may be attributed to the participation of small molecules derived from the degradation of lignocellulose in the synthesis of M-CDs. It was suggested that the pretreatment of lignocellulosic feedstock with DES enhanced the optical properties of the resulting CDs.

To determine the fluorescence lifetime of CDs, time-resolved spectra were further tested. Fig. S5 shows the measured CDs lifetimes of 3.74 ns, 5.78 ns,6.52 ns, 5.91 ns, and 6.67 ns, respectively. Differences in fluorescence intensity can be elucidated through XPS analysis. Table 2 and S1 reveal that M-CDs possess higher C/O ratios than M-DES-CDs, which were associated with the involvement of lignin with higher carbon content in the construction of CDs. A high C/O ratio typically led to the formation of larger graphitic carbon domains with a more pronounced degree of graphitization (Mintz et al., 2020) These domains, characterized by a higher proportion of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms, resulted in enhanced π-π conjugated structures. This increased the probability of radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs, thereby strengthening fluorescence emission. In general, the establishment of an appropriate DES system can synthesize CDs with remarkable properties in the deconstruction of lignocellulose.

Recyclability is a distinguishing characteristics of DES as green solvents, and evaluating the reusability of DES is essential for the advancement of sustainable fractionation. In this study, the reuse of ReDES was also critical for the synthesis of CDs. Two distinct types of DES were employed to treat balsa, resulting in the ReDES system. The structure of ReDES was further investigated using FTIR and NMR. Enhanced absorption peaks at approximately 1100 cm−1 and 1700 cm−1 were observed in both ReDES systems (Fig. S6), which indicated an increase in the content of carbohydrate or lignin fragments. The result was further supported by the identification of 1H and 13C NMR (Figs. S7 and S8), where new impurity peaks emerged with increasing reuse. The impurity peaks observed in the 1H NMR spectrum between 1.0 and 2.5 ppm and in the 13C NMR spectrum between 15 and 50 ppm were likely attributable to the presence of aliphatic alkanes within the lignin. Furthermore, the chemical shift of A-ReDES at 6.8 ppm in the 1H NMR spectrum suggested the structural characteristics of the lignin aromatic region, indicating the dissolution and accumulation of lignin fragments. During several reuse processes, the carbohydrate and lignin fragments within ReDES accumulated and decomposed, providing a high-quality carbon source for the synthesis of CDs. Furthermore, prolonged exposure to elevated temperatures induced the decomposition and polymerization of certain DES small molecules, which also contributed to the formation of CDs.

The fluorescence spectra of CDs obtained by ReDES are shown in Fig. 5a-b. Both types of CDs exhibited a noticeable redshift in wavelength as the number of cycles increased, although their intensity variations differed significantly. These differences may be attributed to their distinct processing environments and interactions. As the number of DES cycles increased, a greater quantity of degradation products from lignocellulosic biomass, along with small molecules derived from DES were incorporated into the CDs formation. This incorporation resulted in a more regular structural formation of the CDs and increased elemental doping, thereby enhancing their fluorescence intensity (Miao et al., 2018). The increased intensity of A-CDs may also be linked to a decrease in Al3+ as the number of cycles increased (Table S2). This occurred because certain metal ions, such as Al3+, can chelate with the CDs and diminished their fluorescence properties (Li et al., 2022b). Furthermore, the increased carbon-to-oxygen ratio, which led to a higher degree of graphitization also enhanced fluorescence intensity. As for E-CDs, the number of functional groups increased with the number of cycles, leading to larger particle size and a redshift in emission wavelength (Fig. S9). However, this occurred at the expense of fluorescent intensity. In Fig. S9, the CD structure appeared homogeneous and nearly spherical, with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm in the crystal structure. Thus, continuous treatment had minimal impact on the (100) crystal structure of the CDs, with only alterations in particle size observed. FTIR spectral analysis, depicted in Fig. S10, reveals that the CDs derived from the same system exhibit similar absorption peaks. Absorption peaks at 1380 cm−1 and 1120 cm−1 corresponded to C-H and C-O-C bonds, respectively, and gradually increased with cycles. This suggested a great number of lignocellulose-degraded small molecules were involved in the construction of the CDs. The presence of specific functional groups on the surface of CDs was further confirmed by XPS analysis, as shown in Tables S1 and S2. The tables illustrate a gradual increase in the total content of CC in A-CDs, indicating a larger conjugated sp2-domain within the particles. This phenomenon contributed to the reduction of the energy gap in the CDs, consequently inducing a shift in the wavelength of PL emission toward the red region (Qu et al., 2016). In contrast, the C/O ratio of E-CDs decreased with increasing reuse, while the total CO content increased. This trend suggested a higher involvement of oxygen-containing functional groups in the synthesis of CDs, leading to the formation of more surface defects. Such changes led to a narrowing of the energy gap in the CDs, thereby promoting red-shifted emission (Bao et al., 2015). Based on these results, it is evident that the performance of the CDs gradually improved with an increasing number of DES cycles. Therefore, appropriately increasing the number of cycles would yield CDs with enhanced performance, fully exemplifying the superiority of this synthesis pathway.

Investigations into the concentration of CDs were conducted to further understand their fluorescence characteristics. Fluorescence exploration was carried out using two distinct DES systems, acidic DES (A-DES) and alkaline DES (E-DES). Fig. 5c-d display the fluorescence spectra of CDs at various concentrations when excited at 365 nm. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity of both types of CDs initially increased and then decreased with rising concentration. The emission wavelength gradually shifted towards longer wavelengths with increasing concentration. This phenomenon was supposed to aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), which results from quantum size effects caused by agglomeration (Yang et al., 2019). To be specific, as the concentration increased, CDs tended to aggregate, leading to the formation of larger sizes and eventual settling. The fluorescence performance was ultimately diminished by this aggregation. The majority of CDs exhibited the ACQ phenomenon (Jing et al., 2023). Thus, it can be inferred that CDs produced under mild conditions of DES possess similar fluorescence characteristics to those synthesized under typical high-temperature and high-pressure conditions. High thermal stability and photobleaching resistance are essential for the application of CDs in various fields. The thermal and photostability of M-CDs and A-CDs were analyzed, as shown in Fig. S11. The fluorescence intensity exhibited minimal variation when the temperature was increased from 0 °C to 160 °C. Moreover, after continuous irradiation with UV light (365 nm) for 480 minutes, the fluorescence intensity of CDs showed minor fluctuations. These findings indicated that the prepared CDs possess excellent photoluminescent thermal stability and resistance to photobleaching, making them highly reliable for applications in anti-counterfeiting encryption and other related fields.

Cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin were treated with A-DES and M-DES, respectively, for the synthesis of CDs. Comparisons were made between the CDs synthesized using lignocellulose components and those synthesized using DES. Fig. 6a-b present the fluorescence spectra of CDs synthesized using different precursors obtained from DES systems. At equivalent concentrations, A-H-CDs exhibited elevated fluorescence intensity, while A-L-CDs demonstrate a greater emission wavelength. Conversely, in the context of M-DES, M-L-CDs displayed enhanced intensity and wavelength characteristics. This phenomenon can be attributed to the larger π-electronic domain or higher graphite N content associated with lignin-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 6c and d, the CDs derived from different lignocellulosic components exhibited comparable UV absorption peaks that aligned with their respective structural and fluorescence properties. In particular, L-CDs exhibit notable π-π* and n-π* electron transitions that were intimately connected to the aromatic structure of lignin.

In order to further investigate the optical characteristics of CDs, FTIR and XPS analyses were conducted. In Fig. S12, lignocellulose CDs and DES-CDs exhibit similar functional group structures, with the main difference observed in the region between 1430 cm−1 and 1530 cm−1, corresponding to the C-H bond in lignocellulose. Particularly, the M-L-CDs exhibited prominent peaks at 1504 cm−1 and 1462 cm−1, which were related to the aromatic backbone vibrations of lignin. This observation suggested a high involvement of lignin in the formation of these CDs. Notable differences in the recovery rates of residue and the yields of CDs were observed across different lignocellulosic components, as indicated in Tables S3 and S4. The acidic DES caused extensive degradation of numerous components, resulting in the formation of smaller molecules, which in turn enhanced the yield of CDs. In contrast, under alkaline conditions, the degradation of cellulose and hemicellulose was less pronounced, with recovery rates of 97.50 % and 87.00 %, respectively. Lignin, however, was significantly degraded, resulting in a recovery of only 58.00 %, yet this process led to a higher yield of CDs at 5.04 %. Therefore, it can be inferred that the synthetic precursors of CDs obtained from the treatment of lignocellulose with acidic and alkaline DES were distinct. Alkaline M-CDs were likely primarily relied on lignin as a carbon source, while the formation of acidic A-CDs may be ascribed to the involvement of a variety of lignocellulosic sources. XPS analysis of the various elements in CDs obtained from DES systems is presented in Tables S5 and S6. The results indicated that L-CDs derived from both acidic and alkaline DES systems exhibited a high C/O ratio, which can be attributed to the aromatic structure of the precursor lignin. Furthermore, the graphite N and CO contents of L-CDs were higher than those of C-CDs and X-CDs. These results suggested that L-CD may possess more ordered crystal structure domains and more functional groups compared to C-CD and X-CD. The sacrifice in intensity made by the A-L-CDs led to a longer wavelength that was consistent with this observation (Fig. 5a).

Based on these results, the synthesis mechanisms of acidic A-CDs and alkaline M-CDs were speculated. In the A-DES system, the use of AlCl3‧6 H2O as a Lewis acid promoted the formation of hydrogen bonds between chloride ions and glycerol through electron transfer and electrostatic attraction (Islam et al., 2022). Simultaneously, Al3+ were coordinated by six water molecules, some of which were replaced by glycerol hydroxyl groups as ligand binding sites. This interaction resulted in the formation of Al-O ligand complexes with Al3+, which promoted the release of H+ ions. During the deconstruction of lignocellulose, A-DES was employed to disrupt the ether bond within lignin, the ester bonds connecting lignin and carbohydrates, the hydrogen bonds in cellulose and hemicellulose, as well as the glycoside bonds (Satlewal et al., 2018; Zdanowicz et al., 2018). The possible mechanism of A-CDs synthesis is shown in Fig. 7a. The carbohydrate and lignin fragments produced by DES in the deconstructing of lignocellulose were dissociated and degraded into oligomers and small molecules at high temperatures (Qiu et al., 2021; Teboho and John, 2020; Wu et al., 2021; Yan and Qi, 2014). These compounds then underwent ring opening, dehydration, condensation, and chain-breaking reactions, leading to the formation of a range of heterocyclic compounds with polycyclic rings (Zhu et al., 2022b). Aromatization and carbonization reactions occurred gradually over time and eventually, aromatic clusters form CDs through burst nucleation (Xue et al., 2023). In the DES system, if additional small molecules were involved, such as compounds containing nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), and other elements, they would also react with the lignocellulose-degrading small molecules to construct the structure of CDs. This may result in the doping of elements and enhance luminescent performance. XPS analysis verified the presence of Al element doping in A-CDs, predominantly in the form of Al-O bonds. However, the content of Al gradually diminished or even vanished when the DES was reused. This observation suggested that the distribution of the Al element was primarily localized on the surface of CDs. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity also gradually increased during this process. To further investigate the role of the Al element in CDs, the A-CDs aqueous solution was treated with EDTA and Al3+, respectively, to detect the fluorescence properties of CDs. As illustrated in Fig. S13, the fluorescence intensity of EDTA-CDs was enhanced, whereas that of Al3+-CDs experienced a minor reduction. Hence, it is reasonable to speculate that Al3+ on the surface of CDs interacted with carboxyl, hydroxyl, and other functional groups to form complexes, thereby diminishing the inherent fluorescence properties of the CDs. The initial complex structure of the CDs was disrupted by the introduction of EDTA, which liberated Al3+ that subsequently formed a new complex with EDTA. This led to a discernible increase in the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. The above analysis indicated that the use of different DES resulted in distinct patterns of elemental doping. Specifically, nitrogen doping improved the fluorescence performance of CDs, whereas aluminum doping had a negative impact on these properties. In summary, the selection of the DES system and the consequent doping of elements are important factors that significantly affect the optical properties of CDs.

C-CDs, H-CDs, and DES-CDs exhibited similar fluorescence properties within the M-DES system, further indicating that cellulose and hemicellulose contributed minimally to the synthesis of M-CDs (Fig. 6b). This finding revealed that during the M-DES treatment of lignocellulose, a protective effect was observed on the structures of cellulose and hemicellulose. Specifically, under mildly alkaline conditions and low temperatures, these components primarily underwent a weak peeling reaction, thereby preserving their structural integrity (Knill and Kennedy, 2003). This mild treatment hindered their potential as carbon precursors for the synthesis of CDs. In contrast, lignin played a vital role in the production of M-L-CDs, which exhibited favorable fluorescence properties. Consequently, lignin emerged as the primary carbon precursor for the formation of M-CDs during the deconstruction of lignocellulose by M-DES, whereas cellulose and hemicellulose contributed minimally to the synthesis process. The possible mechanism of M-CDs synthesis was shown in Fig. 7b. During the pretreatment process, the lignin-carbohydrate complex (LCC) and the hydrogen bonding network within lignocellulose were disrupted, yielding small molecular fragments. These fragments interacted with M-DES molecules, leading to their polymerization into polymer clusters on both horizontal and vertical planes. Over time, these clusters gradually increased in size, underwent carbonization and aromatization processes, and ultimately formed CDs (Cao et al., 2022). In conclusion, while CDs can be obtained through the deconstruction of lignocellulose using both acidic and alkaline DES, the primary carbon precursors and synthesis mechanisms are not consistent.

The progressive accumulation of metal ions in the environment poses a potential hazard to public health. Consequently, it is necessary to monitor metal ions. Fluorescence sensing capabilities of M-CDs towards various metal ions in aqueous solutions were examined at room temperature. As depicted in Fig. 8a, a notable decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed for the Fe3+-CDs solution, whereas other ionic mixed solutions showed no significant changes. This observation provided evidence for the effectiveness of M-CDs in detecting Fe3+. The abundance of amine groups in the synthesized CDs may account for this phenomenon, as they form specific covalent bonds with Fe3+, leading to fluorescence quenching (Table S1) (Shi et al., 2016). Furthermore, Fig. 8b illustrates the fluorescence spectra of CDs at varying concentrations of Fe3+, showing a distinct decrease in fluorescence intensity as the concentration increased. This indicated that M-CDs demonstrated a high sensitivity towards Fe3+. The Stern-Volmer plot (Fig. 8c) further characterizes Fe3+ sensing, revealing a linear relationship between fluorescence decrease (F0/F) and Fe3+ concentration (0–200 ppm), with a linear regression equation of F0/F = 0.0405X + 1.2855 and a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9834 (Singh et al., 2020). The LOD of the synthesized CDs was determined to be 191 ppm.

Table 3 provides a comparison of LOD values for other photoluminescent materials. The LOD values obtained in this study are relatively low, suggesting the potential of the synthesized CDs for ion detection applications. Moreover, it is noteworthy that most previous studies on CDs synthesis involved highly reactive chemicals and costly equipment (Yue et al., 2019). In contrast, this study offers significant advantages by employing a sustainable carbon precursor and a straightforward, environmentally friendly, and cost-effective approach.

In recent years, the rise of counterfeit products has increased the demand for anti-counterfeiting technologies. Additionally, the protection of sensitive information to prevent data breaches and enhance information security has become an urgent issue. To address these concerns, PVA-M-CDs and PVA-A-CDs inks were prepared by complexing PVA with M-CDs and A-CDs, respectively. The PVA-M-CDs ink exhibited excellent dispersion characteristics and maintained remarkable thermal stability when subjected to temperatures ranging from 0°C to 140°C (Fig. S14). These two inks were applied to a template and air-dried in a repeated process to achieve fluorescent patterns. Fig. 8d depicts these patterns on black cardboard, showing that both PVA-CDs and PVA inks appear similar under sunlight but distinct differences under 365 nm UV irradiation. Specifically, the patterns marked “B” and “F” with PVA-M-CDs ink, and the letter “J” written with PVA-A-CDs ink, appear notably brighter. In contrast, the letter “U” obtained with PVA ink appears virtually imperceptible. These observations indicated that the three inks exhibited distinct luminescent properties when exposed to 365 nm excitation. Notably, a prolonged afterglow phenomenon, lasting up to 1.4 s, was observed upon switching off the UV excitation ( Fig. 9a). This significantly enhanced the encryption level and rendered it highly suitable for applications related to information storage and anti-counterfeiting measures. Subsequently, composite films were fabricated using two types of PVA-CDs inks, with their luminescence patterns and spectra depicted in Fig. 9b and c. The maximum fluorescence emission of the PVA-M-CDs and PVA-A-CDs composite films was 463 nm and 442 nm, respectively, under 365 nm irradiation. Upon turning off the 365 nm excitation, phosphorescence emission was observed at 487 nm and 506 nm, respectively. The phosphorescence lifetimes (Table S7) of the composites were recorded as 214 ms and 194 ms, respectively. This result was consistent with the afterglow phenomenon observed in Fig. 9a and b. Aqueous solutions of CDs do not exhibit phosphorescence, whereas PVA-CDs inks and film composites demonstrated robust phosphorescent emission properties. This behavior was attributed to the encapsulation of CDs within the PVA matrix, which restricted non-radiative transitions of CDs and facilitates the generation of phosphorescence (Wang et al., 2021; Zhai et al., 2021). Moreover, Ginkgo biloba and four-leaf clover patterns obtained using similar procedures suggested the potential of PVA-CDs as highly effective fluorescent ink.

M-CDs can be vacuum impregnated in BC to prepare BC-CDs composite films. As depicted in Fig. 8e, the composite films exhibit distinct photoluminescent properties. Under sunlight and 365 nm UV excitation, the two composite films displayed significant differences in emission. The BC film appeared white in sunlight and purplish-blue under UV illumination. In contrast, the BC-CDs composite film exhibited a light brown color under sunlight and a light green color under UV excitation. To further investigate the effect of M-CDs incorporation on the surface morphology and elemental composition of the BC film, SEM and EDS analyses were performed on both composite films. According to Fig. S15, the fibers of both films are interwoven and exhibit no significant differences. Additionally, the elemental distribution of C, O, and N (Table S8) within both composite films was widespread and comparable, indicating that the incorporation of CDs minimally affected the surface structure of the BC films. It was observed that the incorporation of M-CDs led to changes in the elemental composition of the BC film, with an increased C/O ratio, further confirming the successful doping of high-carbon-content CDs into the BC film. This analysis demonstrated the broad application prospects for solutions of CDs as aqueous fluorescent dyes. The prepared BC-CDs composite materials exhibited substantial potential for applications in anti-counterfeiting, encryption, and information storage.

In conclusion, an effective and promising method was successfully developed for synthesizing CDs through the treatment of lignocellulose using various DES systems. The synthesized CDs, rich in surface functional groups, demonstrated superior fluorescence properties, with the maximum fluorescence emission of the CDs obtained from the m-phenylenediamine/ethylene glycol system at 530 nm. Differences in carbon precursors and synthetic mechanisms were observed for CDs obtained from different DES systems. The synthesis in the aluminum chloride hexahydrate/glycerin system involved a complex interplay of lignocellulosic components, while the m-phenylenediamine/ethylene glycol system predominantly utilized lignin. Furthermore, recycling DES optimized the photoluminescent properties of the CDs. The CDs also showed favorable dispersibility and photostability, enabling the selective detection of metal ions, with a LOD of 191 ppm for Fe3+. Additionally, CDs can be integrated with PVA and BC to produce photoluminescent inks and composite films, suitable for applications in information storage and anti-counterfeiting encryption. This study proposes a new approach for the green synthesis of CDs through DES treatment of lignocellulose.
